from Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs and other Disney specials. The Mirror spent the
entire show talking about why the VILLAINS are the reason why Disney stories ár. ,o ,popular, along with clips of various Disney films to prove his point. To paraphrase what

what do you got? Boredom. It's the villain that determines how much you like the good

guys. And, it's the üllain that is #1 in any story. And guess rvhat? It's the exact same in
your emails. And, when you understand this, it will make your emails a lot more
profitable, with a lot less effort, and a lot more fun to write.
Here's what I mean:
Regardless of what you sell (product, service, fundraising etc doesn't matter) or
who you sell to, your product is the hero, riding in on his noble steed and slaying the bad
guy But, if the bad guy is not seen as a truly credible threat, something eül and
malicious, and that will destroy the world (or, rather, the prospect's world) then your
product will not look nearly as eflective as it really is. Like in Disney moües, the üllain
defines the hero, not vice versa. Without an eüI, powerful, diabolical villain, the hero is
just another ordinary chump walking the streets. And, thus, if you spend a lot of time
defining the problem (the üllain) in your emails, your solution will look like a badass hero
with a cape even if he's a 1i0 lb weaklingjust for challenging said villain in the first place.
This is why it can be so profitable to write emails in such a way where you get very
detailed (almost to the point of obnoxiousness) about a problem, but then be very vague
about the solution (the hero). Without that üllain, 1,our hero has no purpose. It has no
context. And, it has no reason to exist at all- much less be bought. No*, villains can
take all kinds of forms. The easiest ones to write are physical pain. Those villains are very
canned and easy. Think Lex Luthor. You know he's a üllain. You know he is plotting to
blow up the world with you on it. And, you know he must be stopped. But, there are other
kinds of üllains, too, depending on your market. For example, there's loneliness,
emotional painlangst, humiliation, failure, fear, psychological pain, embarrassment, and

the list goes on. That's why, one of the most profitable things you can do is create a sort of

Subject line: Bevrare the sociopath next door
One frightening truth about the world is that there are rea}ly, really,
REAILY bad people in it.
In fact, one harvard psychologist has documented that L out of 23
people in the worl-d -- or 4Z of the population -- is what,s called a
"sociopath".
What exactly is a sociopath?
A sociopath is simpry someone who has no conscience. someone who !
doesn't have the ability to bond with another person or even know what
it's like to care about another human being.
It's sort of like people who are color blind.
They know there's a color carled "red". because peopre have told them
there is. But they have never actually seen it and can't even imagine
what it looks like.
And that's how it is with sociopaths when it comes to caring about
other peopre. Their minds simply aren't "wired" to feel compassion.

trhy?

Another Ernail Marketing
Gold Brick Frorn Donald Trurnp
How about a little inspiration for your righteous self? This is for anyone who
knows they should be mailing daily, and hasn't. Or, worse, someone still waiting to start
mailing daily. Anyway, while reading Donald tump's magnificent 'i\rt Of The Deal"
book (one of the best business-related books I ever done read) he says something that,
when translated into what we do (sending emails that make money), is worth sotid gold
bar bricks falling out of the sky:
"Most people who do have the instinct Ifor making deals] wiII never
recognize that they do, because they don,t have the courage or the good
fortune to dj-scover their potential. Somewhere out there are a few men
with more innate talent at golf than Jack Nicklaus, or \¡romen with
greater ability at tennis than Chris Evert or Martina Navratilova, but
they will never lift a club or swing a racket and therefore will never
find how great they could have been. Instead, they'11 be content to sit
and watch stars perform on tel-evision."
I could say the same about a lot of people online. How many people are on the
fence about getting serious about email... who have talent and instinct for this sort of
thing... but never do it. Never even try. Or, at most, write one email per month and never
let themselves have fun with it, throw their inhibitions away (about w-hat people will -

The 55Get Off My Lawn!" Ernail
Over the past year especially, there's been this new fad amongst the marketing
fanboys to tell people to get off their list. The ones I see sound not only pathetic and
needy (like when someone is trying to act "tough" when scared and pretending not to be,
but everyone can see right through it), but are destroying their reputation, their brand,
and, yes, their sales (to the people who see it, at least). It's as stoopid as when people on
flakebook drama queen and grandstand about how they are cleaning house and getting
rid of friends... instead of just doing it. Mostly, the reason this is the case is because they
are using the "get off my list" email as a tactic and not a righteous principle by which
they do business. When I say something urgng certain people to opt out it's not to be a
dick or whatever. It's because there are certain people I really don't want on my list. I
want to repulse them away. But, I want to do it in a way that makes sense not in away
that makes me look like a jackass. Contrast to most of these types of emails where they
sound way too much like they're trying too hard. I\o*, if you're tryirg to repulse people
off your list correctly, it will happen natura\ as a way of writing using my not-so-secret
methods. Mailing daily (or more than once daily). Being a personality. Talking about
controversial things (and having a strong opinion - taking a stand). Calling out the
frauds. Dispensing tough love. And the list goes on. But, if you're really itching to get
some people off your list, then do this: Do a q&A email (see the "Email Player's
Playbook") answering someone's complaining (any complaint will do), and talk to that
one person about opting out if they don't like you or what you're saying. That way,
everyone gets to eavesdrop on the conversation. It also doesn't sound like you're
grandstanding, drama queening or lecturing everyone (including the people who you
want to keep around). Plus, anyone who agrees with the complainer will hopefully leave,
too.
Aryuruy, go ahead and tell someone to get off your lawn..fust don't tell the whole
neighborhood about it while standing on your rooftop wearing a clown costume - which
is pretty much what most people look like when they tell everyone to opt out.

of this newsletter. But, I'm a big fan of ugly. In fact, not only has ugly always made me
more sales, but many of my business and marketing "heroes" (both online and offline)
have all been big fans of ugly, simple, and non-"cool" Iooking. This goes for everything
from emails and sales letters, to product packaging and entire websites. Below are a few
examples for you to chew on. Especiully if you have been stressing over payrng a lot of
money for fancyJooking websites, or taking way too long to get something up and tested.
Check these wrinkly looking figs out:
. Drudge Report - The Drudge Report ftmv.DrudgeReport.com) has got to be the
ugliest, Ieast fancy, and most "amateur" looking website on the entire Internet. It's
some kind of one-color courier font (all bold). The layout is crude. There are minimal
graphics (usuallyjust some photo at the top that is inherently entertaining). And, it's so
butt fugly it looks like a l2-year old built it with the old Netscape Composer software.
(Frankly, it probably was). But you know what? It's also THE #1 leading source of
referral trafñc (excluding social media and search referrals) on the Internet. Something
like 87% of üsitors stay there for 30 seconds (and it's not unusual for people to leave
their browser on watching it as it refreshes itself through out the day). It gets over 2
million visitors per day. And, This is despite it's fugliness and that there is nothing
really "original" about it. It's just a news aggregator that pulls stories from other sites.
The only skill put into it is (1) clarity (it's very clear and easy to read) and (2) the
headlines. Think on that next time you're going to spend all day and a fortune on
something fancy.
. The lateJirn Straw - I'm a hugeJim Straw fanboy. I have patterned many of my
business philosophies, email methods, and marketing actions after what I learned from
him over the years. He died a few years back. But, his site is still up (and probably
selling I don't know). He was always known as one of the most prolific and respected
marketers on the planet. He sold tons and tons of info products of all prices, and r,vas
a helluva afñliate marketer, too. But guess what? His site is even uglier than Drudge. It

lools like it was build in 1994 and never updated (he doesn't use a table to center the

PayPal. Had no refund guarantee. Didn't even fugk around with social media (he was
on Facebook but I don't recall him using it much). He mailed daily with plain text ugly

than that? The guy was a true genius. And yet, he was as barebones as it came. You

Gene Schwartz - One of the best direct mail copy.writers ever to walk the earth.
He said he "specialized" in ugly. He once astutely pointed out: "The ugly thing in a
world of beauty stands out." FIe admitted he was a HUGE fan of ugly layouts. The
uglier, the better. The more random, the better. Why? Because they stood out. And
what stands out is more likely to get noticed. And what's noticed is more likety to be
bought from. (Assuming your copy is good...) This is one reason why I do ugly emails
(other than the extra sales, speed, and simplicity, of course). It's also why I dá things
Iike put the entire "http:/ /wwtv..." in my URLs in emails, too. It sticks out. It is far
more likely to be noticed by a passive reader (reading is primarily a passive actiüty).
And, if someone prints the emails out (especially baby boomers and older - which is
a big segment of my buyers) they can go to the site knowing exactly what it is.
Hopefully you're seeing a pattern here, but here are a few more just in case:
Dan Kennedy - Many years ago I wrote some articles for a guy who had some kind
of licensing deal to sell Dan Kennedy's l{O BS Newsletter back issues. One of the
perks of that gig was getting to read a lot of those back issues. Anyway, in one of the
articles I read, Dan Kennedy talked about how they sold some expensive course on
success (something like that) and it was all prettied-up, slick, and "valuable" looking.
The result? Lots and lots and lots of refunds. So they decided to make the product
ugly - photocopied pages in a binder. No bells. No whistles. The kind of format
that'd make a designer weep and gnash her teeth. What happened? Refunds dropped
like a politician's pants at a brothel. Same with ebooks. They're digital ((air,,
- with
no tangible value. Many of them are even more plainJane than my stuff yet,
they often command fi27 , §37,947 or more... while in a book store that same
information, all dolled up and designed, would not get even g15, most likely. There
are exceptions to this, of course. Like, for example, Gury Bencivenga's DVD course.

But it's yet another reason to test ugly. Or, at the very least, not let haüng ugly stop

. My Own Results - I can tell you, in my own businesses over the years ugly and
simple and bare bones has always won. Even in weight loss, where everything is pretty,
lots of graphics, before-and-after photos, etc. We had none of that - not even a
single before-and-after pic. Yet,Jim Yaghi (who did the traffic for the business) told me
we converted 40o/o of the list to buyers. Fo.ty percent. Almost half. I'm not saying
there wasn't room for improvement, but that's not too shabby. This goes for sales
letters (including a üdeo sales letter), emails, products, and everyüing. Ugly may not
look like much. But it is fast, it's efficient, and it's profitable. It also stands out.
